CBS programs are shown outside the United States: through various Paramount Skydance international networks and/or content agreements, and in two North American countries, through U.S.-based CBS stations. [Sky News](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News") broadcasts the *CBS Evening News* on its channels serving the United Kingdom, Ireland, Australia, New Zealand, and Italy.

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Canada")]

In Canada, CBS network programming is carried on cable, satellite, and [IPTV](/wiki/IPTV "IPTV") providers through affiliates and owned-and-operated stations of the network that are located within proximity to the Canada–United States border (such as [KIRO-TV](/wiki/KIRO-TV "KIRO-TV") in [Seattle](/wiki/Seattle "Seattle"); [KBJR-DT2](/wiki/KBJR-TV "KBJR-TV") in [Duluth, Minnesota](/wiki/Duluth,_Minnesota "Duluth, Minnesota"): [WWJ-TV](/wiki/WWJ-TV "WWJ-TV") in [Detroit](/wiki/Detroit "Detroit"); [WIVB-TV](/wiki/WIVB-TV "WIVB-TV") in [Buffalo, New York](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York"); and [WCAX-TV](/wiki/WCAX-TV "WCAX-TV") in [Burlington, Vermont](/wiki/Burlington,_Vermont "Burlington, Vermont")), some of which may also be receivable over-the-air in parts of southern Canada depending on the signal coverage of the station. Most programming is generally the same as it airs in the United States; however, some CBS programming on U.S.-based affiliates permitted for carriage by the [Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission](/wiki/Canadian_Radio-television_and_Telecommunications_Commission "Canadian Radio-television and Telecommunications Commission") by Canadian cable and satellite providers are subject to [simultaneous substitutions](/wiki/Simultaneous_substitution "Simultaneous substitution"), a practice in which a pay television provider supplants an American station's signal with a feed from a Canadian station/network airing a particular program in the same time slot to protect domestic advertising revenue.

### Bermuda

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Bermuda")]

In Bermuda, CBS maintains an affiliation with [Hamilton](/wiki/Hamilton,_Bermuda "Hamilton, Bermuda")-based [ZBM-TV](/wiki/ZBM-TV "ZBM-TV"), locally owned by [Bermuda Broadcasting Company](/wiki/Bermuda_Broadcasting "Bermuda Broadcasting").

### Mexico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Mexico")]

CBS programming is available in Mexico through affiliates in markets located within proximity to the [Mexico–United States border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border") (such as [KYMA-DT](/wiki/KYMA-DT "KYMA-DT")/[Yuma, Arizona](/wiki/Yuma,_Arizona "Yuma, Arizona"); [KVTV](/wiki/KVTV "KVTV")/[Laredo, Texas](/wiki/Laredo,_Texas "Laredo, Texas"); [KDBC-TV](/wiki/KDBC-TV "KDBC-TV")/[El Paso, Texas](/wiki/El_Paso,_Texas "El Paso, Texas"); [KVEO-DT2](/wiki/KVEO-TV "KVEO-TV")/[Brownsville](/wiki/Brownsville,_Texas "Brownsville, Texas")/[Harlingen](/wiki/Harlingen,_Texas "Harlingen, Texas")/[McAllen, Texas](/wiki/McAllen,_Texas "McAllen, Texas"); and [KFMB-TV](/wiki/KFMB-TV "KFMB-TV")/[San Diego](/wiki/San_Diego "San Diego")), whose signals are readily receivable over-the-air in border areas of northern Mexico.

### Central America, the Dominican Republic and the Caribbean

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Central America, the Dominican Republic and the Caribbean")]

In Central America, the Dominican Republic and the Caribbean, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based CBS-affiliated stations or the main network feed from CBS O&Os [WCBS-TV](/wiki/WCBS-TV "WCBS-TV") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") or [WFOR-TV](/wiki/WFOR-TV "WFOR-TV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami"). In addition, network's programming has been available in the [U.S. Virgin Islands](/wiki/U.S._Virgin_Islands "U.S. Virgin Islands") since 2019 on [WCVI-TV](/wiki/WCVI-TV "WCVI-TV") in [Christiansted](/wiki/Christiansted,_U.S._Virgin_Islands "Christiansted, U.S. Virgin Islands") (owned by [Lilly Broadcasting](/wiki/Lilly_Broadcasting "Lilly Broadcasting")).

### Guam

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Guam")]

In the U.S. territory of [Guam](/wiki/Guam "Guam"), the network is affiliated with low-power station [KUAM-LP](/wiki/KUAM-LP "KUAM-LP") in [Hagåtña](/wiki/Hag%C3%A5t%C3%B1a,_Guam "Hagåtña, Guam"). Entertainment and non-breaking news programming is shown day and date on a one-day [broadcast delay](/wiki/Broadcast_delay "Broadcast delay"), as Guam is located on the west side of the [International Date Line](/wiki/International_Date_Line "International Date Line") (for example, *NCIS*, which airs on Tuesday nights, is carried on Wednesdays on KUAM-LP, and is advertised by the station as airing on the latter night in on-air promotions), with live programming and breaking news coverage airing as scheduled, meaning live sports coverage often airs early in the morning.

### Puerto Rico

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Puerto Rico")]

In the U.S. territory of [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico "Puerto Rico"), CBS is carried through a special feed of [Erie, Pennsylvania](/wiki/Erie,_Pennsylvania "Erie, Pennsylvania") affiliate [WSEE-TV](/wiki/WSEE-TV "WSEE-TV"), relayed via [Mayagüez](/wiki/Mayag%C3%BCez "Mayagüez")-based translator W22FA-D.

### South America

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: South America")]

In Ecuador, Peru, Argentina, Venezuela, Colombia and Brazil, many subscription providers carry either select U.S.-based CBS-affiliated stations or the main network feed from CBS O&Os [WCBS-TV](/wiki/WCBS-TV "WCBS-TV") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City") or [WFOR-TV](/wiki/WFOR-TV "WFOR-TV") in [Miami](/wiki/Miami "Miami").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### United Kingdom

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: United Kingdom")]

On September 14, 2009, the international arm of CBS, [CBS Studios International](/wiki/CBS_Studios_International "CBS Studios International"), reached a joint venture deal with [Chellomedia](/wiki/Chellomedia "Chellomedia") to launch six CBS-branded channels in the United Kingdom – which would respectively replace [Zone Romantica](/wiki/Zone_Romantica_(UK) "Zone Romantica (UK)"), [Zone Thriller](/wiki/Zone_Thriller "Zone Thriller"), Zone Horror, and [Zone Reality](/wiki/Zone_Reality_(UK) "Zone Reality (UK)"), as well as timeshift services Zone Horror +1 and Zone Reality +1 – during the fourth quarter of that year.[[77]](#cite_note-77)[[78]](#cite_note-78) On October 1, 2009, it was announced that the first four channels, [CBS Reality](/wiki/CBS_Reality_(UK) "CBS Reality (UK)"), CBS Reality +1, CBS Drama, and [CBS Action](/wiki/CBS_Justice "CBS Justice") (later CBS Justice), would launch on November 16 respectively replacing Zone Reality, Zone Reality +1, Zone Romantica and Zone Thriller.[[79]](#cite_note-broadcastnow-79) On April 5, 2010, Zone Horror and Zone Horror +1 were rebranded as [Horror Channel](/wiki/Horror_Channel "Horror Channel") and Horror Channel +1.[[80]](#cite_note-80)[[81]](#cite_note-81)

[CBS News](/wiki/CBS_News "CBS News") and [BBC News](/wiki/BBC_News "BBC News") have maintained a news-sharing agreement since 2017, replacing the [BBC](/wiki/British_Broadcasting_Corporation "British Broadcasting Corporation")'s longtime agreement with [ABC News](/wiki/ABC_News_(United_States) "ABC News (United States)") and CBS's with [Sky News](/wiki/Sky_News "Sky News") (which would have ended in any event in 2018 due to that entity's purchase by NBCUniversal).[[82]](#cite_note-82)

As of the close of the Viacom merger on December 4, 2019, [Channel 5](/wiki/Channel_5_(UK) "Channel 5 (UK)") is now a sister operation to CBS, though no major changes to CBS's relationship with the BBC are expected shortly, as Channel 5 sub-contracts [its news programming obligations](/wiki/5_News "5 News") to [ITN](/wiki/ITN "ITN").

### Australia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Australia")]

Australian free-to-air broadcaster [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10") has been owned by CBS Corporation since 2017 (subsequently, Paramount Global and currently, Paramount Skydance Corporation). Network 10's channels, [10](/wiki/Network_10 "Network 10"), [10 Comedy](/wiki/10_Comedy "10 Comedy"), [10 Drama](/wiki/10_Drama "10 Drama"), and [Nickelodeon](/wiki/Nickelodeon_(Australian_TV_channel) "Nickelodeon (Australian TV channel)"), all carry CBS programming, with the latter drawing extensively from the wider Paramount Global library including MTV and Nickelodeon. Before the acquisition, CBS had long been a major supplier of international programs to the network. The cost of maintaining program supply agreements with CBS and 21st Century Fox was a major factor in the network's unprofitability during the mid-2010s.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Network Ten entered voluntary administration in June 2017.[[84]](#cite_note-84) CBS Corporation was the network's largest creditor.[[85]](#cite_note-85) CBS Corporation chose to acquire the network, completing the transaction in November 2017.[[86]](#cite_note-86)

### Asia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Asia")]

#### Hong Kong

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: Hong Kong")]

In Hong Kong, the *CBS Evening News* was initially broadcast live during the early morning hours on [ATV World](/wiki/ATV_World "ATV World") and replayed on International Business Channel from [i-CABLE HOY](/wiki/I-CABLE_HOY "I-CABLE HOY"); networks in that country maintain an agreement to rebroadcast portions of the program 12 hours after the initial broadcast to provide additional content in case their affiliates have insufficient news content to fill time during their local news programs.

#### Philippines

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Philippines")]

In the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), *CBS Evening News* is broadcast on satellite network [Q](/wiki/Q_(Philippine_TV_network) "Q (Philippine TV network)") (a sister channel of [GMA Network](/wiki/GMA_Network "GMA Network") which is now [GTV](/wiki/GTV_(Philippine_TV_network) "GTV (Philippine TV network)")), while *CBS This Morning* is shown in that country on [Lifestyle](/wiki/Lifestyle_(Philippine_TV_channel) "Lifestyle (Philippine TV channel)") (now [Metro Channel](/wiki/Metro_Channel "Metro Channel")). Several CBS entertainment programs such as *[CSI](/wiki/CSI_(franchise) "CSI (franchise)")*, *[Late Show with David Letterman](/wiki/Late_Show_with_David_Letterman "Late Show with David Letterman")*, and *[Survivor](/wiki/Survivor_(American_TV_series) "Survivor (American TV series)")* Series are broadcast by [Studio 23](/wiki/Studio_23 "Studio 23") (now [S+A](/wiki/ABS-CBN_Sports_and_Action "ABS-CBN Sports and Action")) and [Maxxx](/wiki/Maxxx "Maxxx"), which are both owned by [ABS-CBN Corporation](/wiki/ABS-CBN_Corporation "ABS-CBN Corporation"). [60 Minutes](/wiki/60_Minutes "60 Minutes") is currently broadcast on [CNN Philippines](/wiki/CNN_Philippines "CNN Philippines") as a part of their Stories block, which includes documentaries and is broadcast on Wednesday at 8:00 p.m. before [CNN Philippines Nightly News](/wiki/CNN_Philippines_Nightly_News "CNN Philippines Nightly News") with replays in a capacity as a stand-alone program on Saturdays at 8:00 a.m. & 5:00 pm and Sundays at 6:00 a.m, all in local time (UTC + 8).

#### India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: India")]

In India, CBS maintained a brand licensing agreement with Reliance Broadcast Network Ltd. for three CBS-branded channels: [Big CBS Prime](/wiki/Big_CBS_Prime "Big CBS Prime"), [Big CBS Spark](/wiki/Big_CBS_Spark "Big CBS Spark"), and [Big CBS Love](/wiki/Big_CBS_Love "Big CBS Love"). These channels were shut down in late November 2013. Following the CBS-Viacom remerger, the Hindi-language general entertainment channel [Colors TV](/wiki/Colors_TV "Colors TV") became a sister network to CBS through the [Viacom18](/wiki/Viacom18 "Viacom18") joint venture with [TV18](/wiki/TV18 "TV18").

#### Israel

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Israel")]

In Israel, in 2012 the channels Zone Reality and Zone Romantica were rebranded as CBS Reality and CBS Drama, respectively. The channels were carried by Israeli television providers [Yes](/wiki/Yes_(company) "Yes (company)") and [Hot](/wiki/Hot_(Israel) "Hot (Israel)"), although as of 2018[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit) they both only carry CBS Reality.

## Controversies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Controversies")]

### "Gay Power, Gay Politics"

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: \"Gay Power, Gay Politics\"")]

On April 26, 1980, CBS aired "[Gay Power, Gay Politics](/wiki/Gay_Power,_Gay_Politics "Gay Power, Gay Politics")", an episode of the *[CBS Reports](/wiki/CBS_Reports "CBS Reports")* documentary series which purported to document the growing political significance of the [LGBTQ community in San Francisco](/wiki/LGBTQ_culture_in_San_Francisco "LGBTQ culture in San Francisco"). The documentary was accused of purposeful misrepresentation and propagating [misinformation](/wiki/Misinformation "Misinformation"), including in its coverage of the [1979 mayoral election](/wiki/1979_San_Francisco_mayoral_election "1979 San Francisco mayoral election").[[87]](#cite_note-87) In spite of the documentary's premise, the episode focused largely on gay sexual practices and among other claims, alleged that 10% of deaths in San Francisco were related to [BDSM](/wiki/BDSM "BDSM"). Journalist [George Crile III](/wiki/George_Crile_III "George Crile III") also attempted to initiate an interview with then mayor [Dianne Feinstein](/wiki/Dianne_Feinstein "Dianne Feinstein") by asking her "how does it feel to be the mayor of [Sodom and Gomorrah](/wiki/Sodom_and_Gomorrah "Sodom and Gomorrah")?" The documentary was aired at a time when the [Christian right](/wiki/Christian_right "Christian right") in the United States sought to use homophobic sentiment as a political tactic.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Six months after the episode aired, the [National News Council](/wiki/National_News_Council "National News Council"), in response to a complaint by journalist Randy Alfred, found that CBS had violated journalistic standards in airing the program.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

### Brown & Williamson interview

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=40 "Edit section: Brown & Williamson interview")]

In 1995, CBS refused to air a *[60 Minutes](/wiki/60_Minutes "60 Minutes")* segment that featured an interview with a former president of research and development for [Brown & Williamson](/wiki/Brown_%26_Williamson "Brown & Williamson"), the U.S.'s third largest tobacco company. The controversy raised questions about the legal roles in decision-making and whether journalistic standards should be compromised despite legal pressures and threats. The decision nevertheless sent shockwaves throughout the television industry, the journalism community, and the country.[[91]](#cite_note-91) This incident was the basis for the 1999 [Michael Mann](/wiki/Michael_Mann_(director) "Michael Mann (director)")-directed drama film, *[The Insider](/wiki/The_Insider_(film) "The Insider (film)")*.

### Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=41 "Edit section: Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy")]

Main article: [Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVIII_halftime_show_controversy "Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy")

In 2004, the [Federal Communications Commission](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission") (FCC) imposed a record $550,000 fine, the largest fine ever for a violation of federal decency laws, against CBS for an incident during its broadcast of [Super Bowl XXXVIII](/wiki/Super_Bowl_XXXVIII "Super Bowl XXXVIII") in which singer [Janet Jackson](/wiki/Janet_Jackson "Janet Jackson")'s right breast (which was partially covered by a piece of nipple jewelry) was briefly and accidentally exposed by guest performer [Justin Timberlake](/wiki/Justin_Timberlake "Justin Timberlake") at the end of a duet performance of Timberlake's 2003 single "[Rock Your Body](/wiki/Rock_Your_Body "Rock Your Body")" during the [halftime show](/wiki/Halftime_show "Halftime show") (produced by then sister cable network [MTV](/wiki/MTV "MTV")).[[92]](#cite_note-92) Following the incident, CBS apologized to its viewers and denied foreknowledge of the incident, which was televised live. The incident resulted in a period of increased regulation of broadcast television and radio outlets (including self-imposed content regulation by networks and syndicators), which raised concerns surrounding [censorship](/wiki/Censorship "Censorship") and [freedom of speech](/wiki/Freedom_of_speech "Freedom of speech"),[[93]](#cite_note-93) and resulted in the FCC voting to increase its maximum fine for indecency violations from US$27,500 to US$325,000.[[94]](#cite_note-Washington_Post_FCC_Final_Fine-94) In 2008, a Philadelphia federal court annulled the fine imposed on CBS, labeling it "arbitrary and capricious".[[95]](#cite_note-95)

### Killian documents controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=42 "Edit section: Killian documents controversy")]

Main article: [Killian documents controversy](/wiki/Killian_documents_controversy "Killian documents controversy")

On September 8, 2004, less than two months before the [Presidential election](/wiki/2004_United_States_presidential_election "2004 United States presidential election") in which he defeated [Democratic](/wiki/Democratic_Party_(United_States) "Democratic Party (United States)") candidate [John Kerry](/wiki/John_Kerry "John Kerry"), CBS aired a controversial episode of *[60 Minutes Wednesday](/wiki/60_Minutes_II "60 Minutes II")*, which questioned then-President [George W. Bush](/wiki/George_W._Bush "George W. Bush")'s service in the [Air National Guard](/wiki/Air_National_Guard "Air National Guard") in 1972 and 1973.[[96]](#cite_note-96) Following allegations of forgery, CBS News admitted that four of the documents used in the story had not been properly authenticated and admitted that their source, Bill Burkett, had admitted to having "deliberately misled" a CBS News producer who worked on the report, about the documents' origins out of a confidentiality promise to the actual source.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) The following January, CBS fired four people connected to the preparation of the segment.[[99]](#cite_note-99) Former CBS news anchor Dan Rather filed a $70 million lawsuit against CBS and former corporate parent Viacom in September 2007, contending the story, and his termination (he resigned as CBS News chief anchor in 2005), were mishandled.[[100]](#cite_note-100)[[101]](#cite_note-101) Parts of the suit were dismissed in 2008;[[102]](#cite_note-102) subsequently in 2010, the entire suit was dismissed and Rather's motion to appeal was denied.[[103]](#cite_note-103)

### Hopper controversy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=43 "Edit section: Hopper controversy")]

See also: [CNET § Hopper controversy](/wiki/CNET#Hopper_controversy "CNET")

In January 2013, [CNET](/wiki/CNET "CNET") named [Dish Network](/wiki/Dish_Network "Dish Network")'s "Hopper with [Sling](/wiki/Slingbox "Slingbox")" digital video recorder as a nominee for the [CES](/wiki/International_CES "International CES") "Best in Show" award (which is decided by CNET on behalf of its organizers, the [Consumer Electronics Association](/wiki/Consumer_Electronics_Association "Consumer Electronics Association")), and named it the winner in a vote by the site's staff. However, CBS division CBS Interactive disqualified the Hopper and vetoed the results as CBS was in active litigation with Dish Network over its [AutoHop](/wiki/AutoHop "AutoHop") technology (which allows users to skip commercial advertisements during recorded programs).[[104]](#cite_note-104) CNET announced that it would no longer review any product or service provided by companies that CBS Corporation was in litigation with. The "Best in Show" award was instead given to the [Razer Edge](/wiki/Razer_USA "Razer USA") tablet.[[105]](#cite_note-wsj-hopper-105)[[106]](#cite_note-pcmag-cnethopper-106)[[107]](#cite_note-verge-cescbs-107) On January 14, 2013, CNET editor-in-chief Lindsey Turrentine said in a statement that its staff was in an "impossible" situation due to the [conflict of interest](/wiki/Conflict_of_interest "Conflict of interest") posed by the lawsuit, and promised to prevent a similar incident from occurring again. The conflict also prompted the resignation of CNET senior writer Greg Sandoval.[[106]](#cite_note-pcmag-cnethopper-106) As a result of the controversy, the CEA announced on January 31, 2013, that CNET will no longer decide the CES Best in Show award winner due to the interference of CBS (with the position being offered to other technology publications), and the "Best in Show" award was jointly awarded to both the Hopper with Sling and Razer Edge.[[107]](#cite_note-verge-cescbs-107)[[108]](#cite_note-108)

### Harassment allegations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=44 "Edit section: Harassment allegations")]

In July 2018, an article by [Ronan Farrow](/wiki/Ronan_Farrow "Ronan Farrow") in *[The New Yorker](/wiki/The_New_Yorker "The New Yorker")* claimed that thirty "current and former CBS employees described harassment, gender discrimination, or retaliation" at CBS and six women accused Les Moonves of harassment and intimidation.[[109]](#cite_note-109) Following these allegations, it was reported on September 6, 2018, that CBS board members were negotiating Les Moonves's departure from the company.[[110]](#cite_note-110)

On September 9, 2018, *The New Yorker* reported that six additional women (in addition to the six original women reported in July) had raised accusations against Moonves, going back to the 1980s.[[111]](#cite_note-111) Following this, Moonves resigned the same day as chief executive of CBS.[[112]](#cite_note-112)

### Second Trump administration

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=45 "Edit section: Second Trump administration")]

In 2025, some accused CBS of capitulating to the second [Donald Trump](/wiki/Donald_Trump "Donald Trump") administration, allegedly in part to gain federal government approval for the [Skydance–Paramount merger](/wiki/Merger_of_Skydance_Media_and_Paramount_Global "Merger of Skydance Media and Paramount Global").

In April, in response to a lawsuit filed by Trump, *[60 Minutes](/wiki/60_Minutes "60 Minutes")* editor Bill Owens resigned, saying "he no longer has control of the show."[[113]](#cite_note-113)

On July 17, [Stephen Colbert](/wiki/Stephen_Colbert "Stephen Colbert") announced that his show, *[The Late Show with Stephen Colbert](/wiki/The_Late_Show_with_Stephen_Colbert "The Late Show with Stephen Colbert")* would be canceled effective May 2026 at the conclusion of his current contract, and that the entire *Late Show* franchise would be retired at that time. While CBS stated that the decision was "purely financial", many were skeptical, since the decision came shortly after Colbert had publicly called CBS's $16 million settlement to Trump "a big fat bribe", the pending Skydance merger was approved soon after by the [FCC](/wiki/Federal_Communications_Commission "Federal Communications Commission"), and Trump publicly celebrated the show's cancellation. Other late night hosts spoke out in support of Colbert, with several appearing in cameo roles on the July 20 episode of *The Late Show* to offer their support.[[114]](#cite_note-114)

On January 14, 2026, CBS reported that Jonathan Ross, the [ICE](/wiki/United_States_Immigration_and_Customs_Enforcement "United States Immigration and Customs Enforcement") agent who shot [Renee Nicole Good](/wiki/Killing_of_Ren%C3%A9e_Good "Killing of Renée Good") in Minneapolis, suffered internal bleeding to the torso after the incident.[[115]](#cite_note-115) A CBS staffer who was unauthorized to speak publicly said, "It was viewed as a thinly veiled, anonymous leak by [the Trump administration] to someone who'd carry it online."[[116]](#cite_note-116) *Forbes*, *The Guardian* and [Mediaite](/wiki/Mediaite "Mediaite") reported that there was "huge internal concern" at CBS News over running that news report.[[117]](#cite_note-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119)

#### Postponed CECOT story

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=46 "Edit section: Postponed CECOT story")]

Main article: [Inside CECOT](/wiki/Inside_CECOT "Inside CECOT")

On December 21, CBS postponed a *60 Minutes* report on the [CECOT](/wiki/CECOT "CECOT") prison in El Salvador three hours before it was due to air. A *CBS News* spokesperson said the segment "needed additional reporting." *[The New York Times](/wiki/The_New_York_Times "The New York Times")* reported that [Sharyn Alfonsi](/wiki/Sharyn_Alfonsi "Sharyn Alfonsi"), who reported the segment, wrote that the segment had been "screened five times and cleared by both CBS attorneys and Standards and Practices"; Alfonsi called the postponement a "political" decision.[[120]](#cite_note-120)

#### Peter Attia resignation over involvement in Epstein files

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=CBS&action=edit&section=47 "Edit section: Peter Attia resignation over involvement in Epstein files")]

In February 2026, [Peter Attia](/wiki/Peter_Attia "Peter Attia"), who joined as part of a new contributor group in January, resigned after U.S. Justice Department documents revealed past email exchanges with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.[[121]](#cite_note-121)
